title: SEGMENT 16 — Target five, the membrane: daptomycin and polymyxins

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Target five, the membrane: daptomycin and polymyxins

The fifth and final target category is the cell membrane itself — and remember my prediction from the beginning: this is the smallest difference between bacteria and us, because both of us have lipid membranes, so these drugs should be our more toxic, last-resort agents.
Let's see if that holds.
Daptomycin is a lipopeptide that works against gram-positive bacteria, including resistant ones like MRSA and VRE.

Its mechanism: it has a fatty tail that inserts into the bacterial cell membrane — a process that requires calcium — and once embedded, daptomycin molecules aggregate and disrupt the membrane, causing it to leak ions.
The membrane potential collapses, the cell can no longer maintain the electrochemical gradients it needs to live, and it dies.
Rapidly bactericidal.
Now here's a mechanism-driven clinical fact that students love because it's so logical: daptomycin cannot be used to treat pneumonia.
Why?
Because in the lungs, daptomycin is bound up and inactivated by pulmonary surfactant — the lipid material that coats your alveoli.
The drug gets sequestered before it can reach the bacteria.
So a perfectly good anti-gram-positive drug is simply off the table for lung infections, purely because of where it ends up.
Mechanism and even tissue chemistry dictating clinical use, one more time.
The polymyxins — polymyxin B and colistin — target gram-negative bacteria.

They're cationic, positively charged molecules that are attracted to the negatively charged lipopolysaccharide of the gram-negative outer membrane; they bind there, disrupt both the outer and inner membranes like a detergent tearing apart a lipid layer, and the cell's contents leak out.
Bactericidal.
And true to the prediction, polymyxins are quite toxic — significantly nephrotoxic, damaging to the kidneys, and neurotoxic.
For decades they were shelved precisely because of that toxicity.

But here's the sobering part, and it sets up our final topic: colistin has come roaring back into use in recent years — not because it got safer, but because we've run out of better options against certain extremely resistant gram-negative bacteria.
We are now reaching for a toxic, sixty-year-old drug as a last resort because the modern, safer drugs no longer work against these organisms.
That is a clinical alarm bell, and it's the perfect transition into where we finish: resistance.
So the prediction held.
Membrane: smallest difference, most toxic drugs, drugs of last resort.
The pattern I gave you early on has now held across all five categories.
Cell wall — biggest difference — safest.
Membrane — smallest difference — most toxic.
Mechanism, all the way down.
